jokes.text: Bra fitter's opening monologue: 'A bra that fits is the single greatest gift that one woman can give another. And if I see either of you two girls wearing one of those napkins by Victoria's Secrets, I will personally come and murder you.'
jokes.setup: She frames bra fitting with sincere, almost spiritual gravitas — the language of a wellness guru.
jokes.punchline: The speech escalates to a murder threat over improperly fitted bras, delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The joke earns its laugh by committing fully to the character's absurd intensity. 'Napkins by Victoria's Secrets' is a genuinely funny mangling of the brand name, and the murder threat as the climax of what is ostensibly helpful advice is a strong escalation. The character is immediately vivid and specific. Just shy of exceptional because it's a recognizable archetype — the terrifyingly passionate specialist — but executed with enough specificity to elevate it.

jokes.text: Ilana introduces Abbi to the bra fitter: 'Look what I brought you... fresh virgin breast meat.'
jokes.setup: A routine introduction between friends at an appointment.
jokes.punchline: Ilana reduces Abbi to a piece of livestock with cheerful, oblivious enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Ilana — completely unaware of how dehumanizing her words are, treating her best friend as a gift-wrapped offering. The economy is exceptional: five words do enormous comedic work. 'Virgin breast meat' is a phrase so wrong in so many directions simultaneously that it achieves its own comedic logic. High character integration because this line belongs only to Ilana.

jokes.text: Abbi asserts she's a 32C; the bra fitter dismisses her: 'You have no idea what you are!'
jokes.setup: Abbi offers her bra size with quiet confidence.
jokes.punchline: The fitter's contemptuous dismissal treats Abbi's knowledge of her own body as laughably naive.
jokes.explanation: The 'you don't know your own body' specialist trope is familiar but executed crisply. The fitter's authority-figure energy makes Abbi's deflation funny. Functional character comedy, not especially original.

jokes.text: The fitter observes Ilana's breast: 'Same inverted nipple on the right breast?' — and Ilana's mother confirms it like a proud parent: 'Of course! She's my little girl.'
jokes.setup: A clinical breast examination — the fitter asks a routine anatomical question.
jokes.punchline: The mother confirms her adult daughter's inverted nipple with maternal pride, as though this is an endearing childhood trait.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two axes: the clinical inappropriateness of the fitter knowing this detail, and the mother's tender, nostalgic confirmation. 'She's my little girl' as a response to a nipple question is a perfectly absurd landing. The rewatch bonus comes from the implication that the mother and fitter have a long shared history with Ilana's body.

jokes.text: 'Bring out the measuring rod!' — followed by Abbi's hesitant 'Okay!'
jokes.setup: The bra fitting is escalating in intensity and intimacy.
jokes.punchline: The 'measuring rod' sounds vaguely threatening/sexual, and Abbi's nervous compliance is the visual punchline.
jokes.explanation: A functional innuendo-adjacent joke that derives most of its comedy from the scene's accumulated absurdity. The phrase itself isn't particularly clever, but it advances the cringe momentum. Abbi's 'Okay!' is the funnier element — her resigned acceptance of whatever this experience is becoming.

jokes.text: The fitter casually drops: 'You have a lump. It is benign.'
jokes.setup: We're in a bra fitting. The fitter is examining Abbi.
jokes.punchline: A potentially terrifying medical revelation is delivered and immediately dismissed in the same breath, then immediately moved on from, as if it's simply part of the fitting process.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the opening sequence. The structure is nearly perfect: the word 'lump' is a horror-movie stinger, and 'It is benign' immediately deflates it — but she moves on without pause, as if she does this every fitting. Maximum funny per word. The joke also carries a secondary layer: what kind of unregulated bra-fitting operation is this? It works as a setup for the whole scene's surrealism. Economy is exceptional — four words accomplish the entire joke. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in terms of sharpness and brevity.

jokes.text: The fitter explains she has to sew two bras together 'because your breasts are very different sizes' — followed by: 'Almost as if they were unrelated, hm?'
jokes.setup: Clinical explanation about Abbi's breast asymmetry.
jokes.punchline: The fitter philosophically muses that Abbi's breasts seem to be strangers to each other — a completely absurd anatomical observation delivered as mild curiosity.
jokes.explanation: The escalating absurdism of this fitting scene peaks here. 'Unrelated' is a great word choice — treating body parts as having social relationships is a productive comedic frame. The trailing 'hm?' adds to the character's oblivious professorial quality. Solidly crafted absurdist character comedy.

jokes.text: The fitter announces Abbi's size: '28KKK' — and Ilana immediately says, 'Just call it an L.'
jokes.setup: The fitter announces an absurdly specific, comically large bra size.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's casual, practical correction — 'just call it an L' — deflates the drama with perfect economy.
jokes.explanation: The triple-K measurement is already funny as an absurdist bra-size joke, but Ilana's instant reframing is the actual punchline. Her 'just call it an L' has the practical wisdom of someone who has been in chaotic fitting rooms many times. The joke is also structurally clean: announcement, beat, deflation. A small, well-executed gag.

jokes.text: Abbi asks: 'Did you insert a back brace without telling me?' after putting on the new bra.
jokes.setup: Abbi has been fitted into what is apparently an architecturally complex bespoke bra.
jokes.punchline: The bra is so structurally extreme that Abbi suspects a medical device was secretly installed in it.
jokes.explanation: A visual gag that works better on screen than on paper — we're presumably seeing Abbi in a dramatic, over-engineered contraption. The line is good but functional. The rewatch bonus is for the visual payoff of seeing the actual bra.

jokes.text: The two bras cost $533. Abbi says 'Holy... expensive' (self-censored), Ilana follows with an unbleeped '(BLEEP) you.'
jokes.setup: The fitter announces the price after a luxurious custom fitting.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's hilariously timid 'Holy... expensive' contrasted with Ilana's immediate and direct profanity at the fitter.
jokes.explanation: The contrast between the two women's reactions is the joke — Abbi's polite near-profanity versus Ilana's unfiltered aggression. 'Holy... expensive' as a deliberate euphemism is unexpectedly funny from Abbi, who rarely codes herself as delicate. Good character differentiation in a small moment.

jokes.text: Abbi snores like a demon. Her roommate filmed it. Abbi denies it, sees the video: 'I sound ill.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is told she snores; she flatly denies it.
jokes.punchline: Video evidence is produced, and Abbi's self-assessment shifts from denial to medical concern — 'I sound ill' — which is both accurate and funnier than a simple admission.
jokes.explanation: A familiar snoring-denial setup elevated slightly by 'I sound ill' — Abbi doesn't move to embarrassment, she moves to clinical concern, which is a slightly unexpected character response. The filming of a sleeping friend is also slightly creepy-funny given the show's friendship dynamic. Functional but not especially original.

jokes.text: Abbi has been waiting for a lightbulb to actually die before replacing it with an energy-efficient one, because throwing out a working bulb 'would be so wasteful.' Her companion calls her 'my eco-warrior princess' and lists her green credentials as: 'energy-efficient bulbs, not showering.'
jokes.setup: Abbi explains her eco-conscious lightbulb-replacement policy with genuine pride.
jokes.punchline: Ilana validates her while immediately undermining the eco-warrior image by equating 'not showering' with environmental virtue — a joke at Ilana's own expense.
jokes.explanation: A nice character-establishing moment that doubles as a joke at Ilana's expense — she genuinely thinks not showering qualifies as environmentalism. The pairing of 'energy-efficient bulbs' with 'not showering' as equivalent eco-warrior credentials is the comedic turn. Good character integration; this reads as authentic Ilana self-mythology.

jokes.text: Ilana finds a Fruit by the Foot wrapper in the ceiling lamp and delivers an elaborate backstory: she hooked up with a 'hot, very old dot-com douche named Brad,' gave him a Waka Flocka Flame ticket, he paid her back in Bitcoin, and the wrapper is the key to her crypto fortune.
jokes.setup: Finding an old wrapper in a lamp — normally unremarkable.
jokes.punchline: The wrapper is a cryptocurrency key, obtained through a chain of transactions involving concert tickets, a dot-com era douchebag, and a Waka Flocka Flame show — each detail more absurd than the last.
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent piece of Ilana character comedy. The specificity of the detail chain — Fruit by the Foot, dot-com douche, Waka Flocka Flame ticket, Bitcoin — is both absurd and completely believable for Ilana's life. Only Ilana would have a Bitcoin fortune hidden in a ceiling lamp in a Fruit by the Foot wrapper obtained via a Waka Flocka transaction. The 'TMI, right? Hate him, loathe him, grossed out by him now, you know?' self-commentary is also very good. Character integration score is very high because no other character on any other show would have this backstory.

jokes.text: Ilana triumphantly announces the password on the wrapper: 'Brad has it.' — then slowly realizes: 'No. Brad has the... Brad has the password. Shit.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been building to revealing the password on the mysterious wrapper.
jokes.punchline: The 'password' written down is not the password but a note that Brad has the password — she's written a message to her future self that only creates a new problem.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed misdirection. The buildup — the elaborate backstory, the 'key,' the password reveal — makes the anticlimax of 'Brad has it' genuinely funny. The slow processing ('No. Brad has the... Brad has the password. Shit.') is perfect comedic pacing; Ilana works through her own stupidity in real time. Strong structure and high character integration — this is exactly how Ilana would handle past-self record-keeping.

jokes.text: Ilana explains that the Bitcoin money is 'not for loans or debt, not rent nor weed or healthcare or any other stupid thing' — listing weed and healthcare as equivalently trivial.
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if Ilana will pay off student loans.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's rejection list lumps together financial necessities (debt, rent, healthcare) and recreational substances (weed) as equally dismissed 'stupid things,' revealing her hierarchy of values.
jokes.explanation: The gag is that healthcare and weed are listed as equivalent luxuries neither worth spending the windfall on. The joke also satirizes millennial broke-but-principled attitudes — the Bitcoin money will be for something grander than survival. Good character integration; this is a precise Ilana worldview statement.

jokes.text: Phone Wigs reveal — Abbi is wearing what turns out to be a phone case with hair on it.
jokes.setup: Ilana mentions her 'wiz-ness' (woman-owned business).
jokes.punchline: We see Abbi is already using a Phone Wig — a phone case with human hair attached — as though it's a completely normal accessory.
jokes.explanation: The Phone Wig concept is a genuinely original comedic invention — a hairy phone case as a product born from misapplied millennial entrepreneurship. Its absurdity is compounded by the fact that Abbi is already using one, treating it as a normal object. A strong visual gag that also establishes a running concept for the episode.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Are you in that dank dungeon that they literally illegally claim to be a laundry room?' and then: 'We're businesswomen, Abb. Time is money.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is reluctant to drop off laundry because it 'feels extravagant.'
jokes.punchline: Ilana — who has no job and no money — lectures Abbi about being a businesswoman for whom time is money, with complete self-seriousness.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's self-designation as a businesswoman who values her time — delivered to a person with an actual job, by someone who has no discernible income — is a good character joke. 'Dank dungeon' is also a nice piece of Ilana vernacular. The irony works because neither character examines it.

jokes.text: Abbi drops off her laundry and announces to the attendant: 'I'm gonna use that time to enrich my life.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been talked into dropping off laundry for the first time.
jokes.punchline: She feels the need to justify the 'extravagance' by declaring high-minded intentions — 'enrich my life' — to a laundromat employee who does not care.
jokes.explanation: A small but accurate character joke — Abbi needs to justify basic self-care as a philosophical act. The gap between the audience (a laundromat worker) and the grandeur of the announcement is the comedy. Clean, character-specific, functional.

jokes.text: Laundromat attendant mishears 'Abbi' as 'Ibbi' and says: 'I like that less.'
jokes.setup: Abbi spells her name, the attendant mishears it.
jokes.punchline: The attendant doesn't correct herself or apologize — she simply volunteers her aesthetic preference for the wrong version.
jokes.explanation: 'I like that less' is economically excellent — four words that communicate the attendant's complete indifference to accuracy and casual contempt for Abbi's actual name. The delivery is the joke: no malice, just blunt aesthetic judgment. A small gem.

jokes.text: Ilana to the Bitcoin broker: 'So, they call it cryptocurrency 'cause it's so damn cryptic. Nobody really gets it.' — delivered as confident expertise.
jokes.setup: Ilana is in a meeting about her Bitcoin, presenting herself as a crypto-savvy businesswoman.
jokes.punchline: Her explanation of the word 'cryptocurrency' is confidently wrong folk etymology — she thinks the name derives from difficulty of understanding.
jokes.explanation: Ilana confidently explaining something she doesn't understand is a reliable comic well for the character. The fake etymology of 'cryptocurrency' is a good specific choice — it's wrong in a way that reveals exactly how much she understands Bitcoin (not at all) while also being the kind of thing that sounds plausible to someone half-listening. High character integration.

jokes.text: The Bitcoin broker steps away to give Ilana privacy; Ilana asks him to stay: 'I feel like we have a good vibe.'
jokes.setup: Broker politely steps out for privacy during a financial transaction.
jokes.punchline: Ilana overrides the social norm by framing a business meeting as a bonding experience — 'we have a good vibe' — wanting him to witness her Bitcoin moment.
jokes.explanation: Small but authentic Ilana behavior — treating professional relationships as personal ones, requiring witnesses to her milestones. Functional character comedy, nothing exceptional.

jokes.text: 'It's like all my favorite memories in one place. Ugh, Dongs.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is scrolling through an old phone looking for Brad's contact info.
jokes.punchline: She finds what appears to be a folder or gallery of dick pics and summarizes it with tender nostalgia — 'Dongs' — as though it's a cherished photo album.
jokes.explanation: The economy here is remarkable — 'Ugh, Dongs' as a wistful sigh communicates an entire relationship history with a phone full of dick pics. The 'Ugh' is perfect: not disgust, not excitement, but the tired affection of someone revisiting an old haunt. Very high character integration; 'Ugh, Dongs' is an Ilana line.

jokes.text: Ilana's strategy for finding Brad's number: 'His dick pic is on one of these. So if I find the dick, I find his number.'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs to contact Brad to get the Bitcoin password.
jokes.punchline: Her proposed solution — cataloguing dick pics until she identifies the right one and extracts a phone number — is presented as a logical deduction.
jokes.explanation: 'If I find the dick, I find his number' is presented with the gravity of a detective's deduction — it's both absurd and has Ilana's own internal logic. The fact that this method apparently works (she finds it) makes it funnier. Strong structure: the premise is shocking, the delivery is matter-of-fact, and the logic is internally consistent.

jokes.text: Ilana immediately finds the relevant dick pic and offers: 'Do you want to see his dick?' The Bitcoin broker flatly declines. She then offers to show him her Phone Wigs instead. He also declines.
jokes.setup: Having located the dick pic, Ilana pivot-offers it as a social gesture.
jokes.punchline: The broker's identical flat refusal to both the dick pic and the Phone Wigs equates them — both are equally unwelcome intrusions.
jokes.explanation: The comedic structure here is a double-refusal that implicitly ranks dick pics and Phone Wigs as equivalent social hazards. The broker's identical 'No' to both is the key — the show doesn't need to editorialize. The Phone Wig being placed in the same category as an unsolicited dick pic is a good running-gag development.

jokes.text: Abbi returns to the laundromat mid-hour to check on her laundry and explains: 'I'm getting a lot done. A lot of work stuff, you know. I'm really productive.'
jokes.setup: Abbi was supposed to use her free hour to 'enrich her life.'
jokes.punchline: She's already back, unable to last the hour, and she lies transparently to the attendant about being productive.
jokes.explanation: A good callback that deflates Abbi's aspiration with predictable but well-executed efficiency. The fact that she came back within the hour to check on laundry she paid someone else to do undercuts everything she said before. Character-specific: Abbi's anxiety expressing itself through obsessive checking.

jokes.text: Abbi enthusiastically checks whether 'the hoodie string stayed in the hole' — identifying it as 'the trickiest part' of laundry.
jokes.setup: Abbi is inspecting her returned laundry.
jokes.punchline: Out of all the laundry results, her primary concern is the hoodie drawstring — an extremely specific, relatable but slightly neurotic laundry anxiety elevated to expert status.
jokes.explanation: An excellent observational-character moment. The lost hoodie string is a universal laundry misery that the show elevates to 'trickiest part' status. Abbi treating it as the marquee test of the laundry service is both specific to her character and broadly relatable. Good economy and character integration.

jokes.text: The laundromat lost Abbi's sweatshirt. Policy explanation: 'We throw all the clothes for the day in a bucket with everyone else's stuff and then we sort them out by memory.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks why her sweatshirt is missing.
jokes.punchline: The laundromat's operational method — unsorted communal bucket, sorted from memory — is revealed as a system so incompetent it's surreal, delivered without apology.
jokes.explanation: The incompetence of the laundromat system is delivered with complete bureaucratic sincerity — they're not apologetic, they're explanatory. 'Sorted by memory' is the kind of absurdist NYC-service-industry detail that feels simultaneously impossible and completely plausible. The deadpan delivery is key.

jokes.text: Laundromat claims 'patient/client privilege' to refuse showing Abbi the client list.
jokes.setup: Abbi asks to see other clients' information to locate her sweatshirt.
jokes.punchline: The laundromat applies doctor-patient confidentiality to laundry service, invoking the legal concept with total confidence.
jokes.explanation: 'Patient/client privilege' is an excellent specific absurdism — it uses real legal language applied to a completely inappropriate context, and its logic is internally coherent enough that it almost sounds reasonable for a second. The laundromat attendant's deadpan insistence on the professional privilege of laundry service is a great character beat.

jokes.text: Abbi, frantically searching for her sweatshirt on the street, approaches a man and accidentally elbows him. She apologizes: 'I lost my virginity in it. You get it.' The man: 'In it?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is showing a stranger a photo of her sweatshirt to see if he's seen it.
jokes.punchline: She overshares the sweatshirt's sentimental significance — losing her virginity in it — as emotional leverage. The stranger's 'In it?' reveals he's focused on the logistical question, not the sentiment.
jokes.explanation: The joke has two good beats: Abbi's TMI overshare escalating from 'lost my virginity' to the stranger's practical clarification of 'In it?' — which prompts Abbi's stumbling explanation ('it was quick'). The stranger's question reframes the sentiment as a logistics question, which is funnier than any response to the emotional content would have been.

jokes.text: Abbi, alone on the street, says to herself: 'My sweatshirt.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has struck out finding the sweatshirt and is standing alone.
jokes.punchline: The camera holds on her saying 'My sweatshirt' with the weight of someone who has lost something precious — the comedy is the disproportionate grief for a piece of clothing.
jokes.explanation: A quiet character beat that works through Abbi's investment in the object. The repeated 'My sweatshirt' at [08:52] after the street failure — separated by a beat from the previous moment — is a good use of the sitcom pause. Economy is high because the simplicity of the line carries all the comedic weight.

jokes.text: The shopkeeper responds to Ilana's 'I am the future' speech by saying 'I've been waiting for this since 1999' — and asks her to spin.
jokes.setup: Ilana has delivered an absurd fashion brief.
jokes.punchline: The shopkeeper responds not with confusion or pushback but with the enthusiasm of someone who has been waiting twenty years for this exact customer.
jokes.explanation: 'I've been waiting for this since 1999' is a good secondary character beat — a shopkeeper who has been anticipating the Matrix-cyberfeminist customer for two decades. The deadpan sincerity of 'give me a spin' follows the logic of a professional who has finally found their ideal client.

jokes.text: Abbi distributing 'Missing Sweatshirt' fliers — a montage of her approaching strangers with the same energy as a missing-person search.
jokes.setup: Abbi has committed to finding the sweatshirt.
jokes.punchline: The visual of Abbi canvassing the neighborhood for a piece of clothing with the urgency and methods of a missing-person search is the sustained joke.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Abbi's response to a lost sweatshirt into full missing-person investigation mode is the backbone of her subplot. The montage structure — identical beats, different strangers — is a clean comedic structure. Sets up the news misunderstanding.

jokes.text: News anchor says to Ilana on camera: 'Ilana Wexler, you haven't changed a bit.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is apparently appearing in a news broadcast.
jokes.punchline: The anchor greets Ilana with the casual familiarity of an old acquaintance — implying Ilana has a prior, unexplained news relationship — as a throwaway detail.
jokes.explanation: A good throwaway that implies a rich, unexplored backstory — Ilana is known to the local news anchor, and this is treated as completely normal. The rewatch value is in noticing this and wondering what their prior encounter was. Economy is good; the joke works in half a second.

jokes.text: Breaking news segment: 'another missing white woman.' The broadcast immediately pivots to breathless crisis coverage with anchor Charlotte Tennaboro.
jokes.setup: Standard local news broadcast.
jokes.punchline: The show pointedly frames 'missing white woman' as a recognized, almost branded news category — 'another' — satirizing the media's disproportionate coverage of missing white women.
jokes.explanation: The 'another missing white woman' framing is a pointed piece of media satire that earns its edge — the joke is about 'Missing White Woman Syndrome,' the well-documented media phenomenon, and the show deploys it with precision. 'Another' is the key word: it's a regular enough occurrence to have a standing news segment. High earned-vs-cheap because the satire has genuine social commentary behind it.

jokes.text: The news reveals the missing woman is the 'fifth employee of Soulstice Gym to go missing in the last four years' — and police have re-opened those cases. Abbi, watching: 'Shit.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is watching a news story about herself as a 'missing person.'
jokes.punchline: The news independently adds dark details Abbi didn't know — she's now the latest in a pattern of Soulstice disappearances — escalating the absurdity while Abbi processes that she's apparently been presumed dead.
jokes.explanation: An excellent escalation of the flier misunderstanding — the news doesn't just report her as missing, it retrospectively folds her into a multi-year disappearance pattern, giving the situation genuine stakes. Abbi's 'Shit' is the right understated reaction. The joke rewards the audience for tracking the setup.

jokes.text: Abbi watching the news and realizing she is 'the missing white woman' — the flier she made for her sweatshirt was mistaken for a missing person flier
jokes.setup: Abbi's missing sweatshirt flier showed her own face
jokes.punchline: The flier's layout caused it to look like Abbi herself was missing. She's now the subject of a breaking news story and a police investigation
jokes.explanation: This is a strong plot-joke — the payoff requires setting up both Abbi's flier campaign AND the 'missing white woman' news beat, and it brings them together perfectly. The joke also works as structural commentary (the flier's layout confusion is itself a joke on Abbi's art school credentials). Earns 8+ because it's a multi-element convergence that rewards attention and has escalating satirical layers.

jokes.text: Soulstice Gym has renamed a bathroom 'Abbi's Room' and retired her plunger in her memory.
jokes.setup: Abbi is watching tributes to herself on the news.
jokes.punchline: The memorial is: a bathroom renamed in her honor and a retired plunger — the gym honoring the cleaning staff's tool as though it were a jersey number.
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. The specificity is exceptional: Soulstice honors Abbi not with a moment of silence or a memorial service but by retiring her plunger — treating it with the reverence of a retired athlete's jersey number. The absurdism is rooted in Abbi's ongoing identity crisis at Soulstice (she's a trainer, not a cleaner, but the gym has always perceived her wrong). The joke also carries genuine character commentary: even in her memorial, she's misidentified as 'a sweet, artistic cleaner.' 'We've also retired her plunger. No!' — Abbi's off-screen shriek 'No!' is a perfect reaction beat. Compares favorably to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: News tribute describes Abbi as 'a sweet, artistic cleaner with a heart of gold.' Abbi: 'I was a trainer when I left. This is unbelievable.'
jokes.setup: Soulstice colleagues are paying tribute to Abbi on the news.
jokes.punchline: The tribute gets Abbi's job title wrong — calling her a cleaner when she was a trainer — and Abbi's indignation focuses on this professional misidentification rather than the fact she's presumed dead.
jokes.explanation: Abbi's priorities are beautifully wrong: not 'I'm not dead' but 'I was a trainer!' This is precise character comedy — Abbi's professional identity at Soulstice has always been contested, and being misidentified in her own memorial is the ultimate insult. High character integration because this joke only works with Abbi's history at that gym.

jokes.text: Soulstice colleague on the news: 'She was my best friend. She was my lover! We were dating.' — Abbi: 'What?!'
jokes.setup: A Soulstice colleague is giving a tearful tribute about Abbi on the news.
jokes.punchline: The tribute escalates from 'best friend' to 'lover' to 'we were dating' — a relationship Abbi had no idea about, which she discovers in real time on television.
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure here is tight: each revelation adds a new dimension — 'best friend' (okay), 'lover' (wait), 'we were dating, yeah' (what). Abbi's 'What?!' between each level is a perfect reaction beat. The joke also layers onto the general indignity of Abbi's situation: not only is she presumed dead by the wrong title, but she's apparently in a relationship she didn't know about.

jokes.text: Abbi calls the news station to clarify she's not missing. The reporter: 'Is she dead? Did you find her body? How many pieces?'
jokes.setup: Abbi calls to correct the record.
jokes.punchline: The reporter's questions escalate from 'is she dead' to 'how many pieces' — the news media's enthusiasm for gore presented as professional procedure.
jokes.explanation: 'How many pieces?' is an excellent dark-comedy question — delivered as a standard triage question from someone who needs to know the severity of the story. The escalation from 'is she dead' to 'how many pieces' in three words is economical dark comedy. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: Abbi tells Inez she's the missing girl and is fine. Inez: 'Are you speaking in code right now? Laugh if you're really okay.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says she's fine and never was missing.
jokes.punchline: Inez refuses to accept the simple truth and applies hostage-negotiation protocols to what is a miscommunication about laundry.
jokes.explanation: 'Laugh if you're really okay' is a genuinely quotable line — it takes the hostage-communication trope ('blink twice if you're in danger') and applies it to a phone call about a fake missing person. The joke is also a good satire of the media treating every story as a thriller regardless of evidence.

jokes.text: After Abbi confirms she's fine, the news team immediately pivots: 'We need another missing-girl story pronto.'
jokes.setup: The crisis is resolved — Abbi is alive.
jokes.punchline: The news team's immediate reaction is not relief but urgency about filling the story slot — missing-girl content is a commodity.
jokes.explanation: A tight callback to the episode's media satire thread. 'Another missing-girl story pronto' reveals that missing-white-woman coverage is demand-driven content production, not journalism. The 'on it' response from the team is the kicker. Sharp, economical, earns its dark comedy.

jokes.text: Abbi explains the layout issue to Inez: 'I made the flier for a missing sweatshirt from art school, and then now I see that it seems as though I was the missing one.' Inez: 'Huh, a layout issue. So I'm guessing you didn't finish art school.'
jokes.setup: Abbi explains the sweatshirt-flier mistake.
jokes.punchline: Inez uses Abbi's design failure as evidence she didn't complete art school — a clean, cutting deduction.
jokes.explanation: A well-structured two-beat exchange. Inez's deduction — 'So I'm guessing you didn't finish art school' — is a sharp, economical insult that follows logically from the evidence. Abbi's defensive clarification ('My concentration was in illustration, not graphic design') makes it funnier by being a real, correct point that completely misses the moment.

jokes.text: Ilana in her 'Bitcoin look' — patent leather outfit — pitches Phone Wigs to Brad: 'This Bitcoin look is patent leather for my impending patent. I am Bitcoin, baby, yas.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is dressed in full cyberpunk patent leather to receive her Bitcoin payout.
jokes.punchline: She narrates her own look with Ilana-specific logic: the patent leather references her patent filing. The visual of Ilana in this outfit is itself the joke.
jokes.explanation: The patent leather/patent pun is classic Ilana: a word association that she treats as a conceptual argument. 'I am Bitcoin, baby, yas' is her self-narrated entrance. The visual payoff (we finally see the outfit) gives the scene a rewatch bonus. High character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana presents the Phone Wig line to Brad: 'We've got the Ross. We've got the Rachel. Going over here to the Lincoln. Aww. Classic Abbi. Aww. Little Jaimé. This time I shit myself... don't ask... and Bernadette Peters, bitch.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is pitching Phone Wigs to her Bitcoin broker by naming each model.
jokes.punchline: The product line names escalate from pop culture (Ross, Rachel) to a presidential Lincoln to an Abbi-branded model to a traumatic personal memory ('I shit myself... don't ask') to Bernadette Peters — a completely chaotic naming system.
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent piece of Ilana character writing. The escalating model names are funny individually, but the sequence as a whole is a masterclass in escalating absurdism: recognizable pop culture references give way to a model named after Abbi (with fondness), then to a personally traumatic memory (casually inserted mid-pitch), then to the non-sequitur of Bernadette Peters. 'This time I shit myself... don't ask' is the standout — it's parenthetical, resigned, and both overshares and withhold simultaneously. 'Bernadette Peters, bitch' as a product name is also great. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in character integration and originality.

jokes.text: Brad, confused: 'T-These are phone cases?' Ilana: 'Brad, I'm sorry. I can't go any slower.'
jokes.setup: Brad has watched the entire Phone Wig pitch and is still struggling to categorize the product.
jokes.punchline: Ilana responds to his basic clarifying question by accusing him of intellectual slowness — she's presented this as clearly as she possibly can.
jokes.explanation: 'I can't go any slower' is an excellent line — Ilana genuinely believes the problem is Brad's comprehension, not her explanation. The humor is in her complete confidence and the fact that her pitch was, by any measure, not clarifying. Economy is high: five words that communicate enormous character.

jokes.text: 'Wiz-ness. My wiz-ness. Wiz-ness!' — Ilana's repeated mantra as a running joke.
jokes.setup: Ilana has been using 'wiz-ness' (woman-owned business) throughout the episode.
jokes.punchline: The word gets its own moment of pure repetition — Ilana chanting it with increasing intensity until it's purely sounds.
jokes.explanation: The running gag of 'wiz-ness' culminates here as a kind of meditation — Ilana has repeated this word so many times it's become pure Ilana-ism. The joke lives in the commitment. Not exceptional but charming.

jokes.text: The sweatshirt is returned to the laundromat — someone saw it on the news. Abbi's reaction: 'Thank you so much.' Then immediately: 'No, I'm kidding. Prank! I've been telling everyone to boycott this place.' The attendant: 'You did what?' Abbi: 'No, I'm kidding. Prank! Please don't come back here. You're a bad person.'
jokes.setup: The laundromat returns Abbi's sweatshirt — apparent happy resolution.
jokes.punchline: Abbi claims she's been running a boycott campaign, then immediately retracts it as a prank — but the attendant's genuine alarm at 'You did what?' and the final 'Please don't come back here. You're a bad person' suggests the attendant is no longer sure what's true.
jokes.explanation: A nice closing beat for the laundromat subplot. The 'Prank!' deflation and the attendant's 'You're a bad person' are a functional exchange. The joke is in the ambiguity — was she actually boycotting? The attendant isn't sure, and neither are we.

jokes.text: Laundry worker: 'Please don't come back here. You're a bad person.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has tried to 'prank' her way out of confessing she organized a boycott
jokes.punchline: The worker's sentence — calm, direct, final — is a perfect judgment delivered without heat. 'You're a bad person' is not hyperbole; it's a verdict.
jokes.explanation: Six words that close the laundromat arc with dignity and precision. 'You're a bad person' from a service worker to a customer — delivered flatly, without anger — is devastating and funny simultaneously. The economy is exceptional.

jokes.text: Abbi tries to give the sweatshirt back to the man she took it from on the street. He refuses: 'I went to RISD. It's too hot out here. I can't wear synthetics.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is returning the sweatshirt to the man she (presumably) retrieved it from.
jokes.punchline: He rejects the vintage sweatshirt on RISD-alumni grounds and fabric elitism — 'I can't wear synthetics' — treating his art school credentials as relevant to sweatshirt acceptance.
jokes.explanation: The RISD credential as context for refusing a free sweatshirt is a very specific New York art-world joke. 'I can't wear synthetics' as the RISD man's position is a precise parody of a particular type of artisanal snob. Also a subtle callback to Abbi's own art school identity, which has been in play all episode.

jokes.text: Abbi offers her recovered sweatshirt to the stranger she elbowed; he went to RISD and can't wear synthetics
jokes.setup: As an apology, Abbi offers her precious sweatshirt — the one she launched a manhunt for — to the stranger she elbowed
jokes.punchline: He rejects it: 'I went to RISD. It's too hot out here. I can't wear synthetics.' — An art school credential and fabric sensitivity make him too refined for the sweatshirt she moved heaven and earth to recover
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback that closes the stranger encounter. The stranger's RISD name-drop is an art school flex that mirrors Abbi's own art school defensiveness earlier. 'I can't wear synthetics' as a class-adjacent rejection of a beloved sweatshirt is perfectly NYC-pretentious. The irony: Abbi — an art school person herself — has her sweatshirt rejected by someone more art-school than her.

jokes.text: Brad finally processes Ilana's backstory aloud: 'Okay, you found your Bitcoin number on an old Fruit by the Foot... that you remember that I gave you for a Waka Flocka Flame ticket... and you want to cash out that money today to get a patent for these Phone Wigs.' Ilana: 'Chugots! Finally. It took long enough, my Brad.'
jokes.setup: Brad is summarizing the situation to confirm he's understood.
jokes.punchline: His summary — which is accurate — sounds completely insane when laid out linearly. Ilana's impatient 'Finally' treats his comprehension as slow rather than her backstory as bizarre.
jokes.explanation: A good callback that lets the audience hear the whole absurd premise restated at once. Brad's incredulous recap makes the comedy of the situation legible again. Ilana's 'Chugots! Finally' positions her as the exasperated teacher. Good structure.

jokes.text: Brad recaps Ilana's entire story back to her: 'Okay, you found your Bitcoin number on an old Fruit by the Foot...' — and Ilana responds 'Chugots! Finally. It took long enough, my Brad.'
jokes.setup: Brad is summarizing Ilana's chaotic backstory to confirm he's understood it
jokes.punchline: 'Chugots!' (Ilana's invented affirmation) followed by 'It took long enough, my Brad' — as if Brad is the slow one for needing the summary repeated back, not her for having the world's most confusing Bitcoin arrangement
jokes.explanation: 'My Brad' as a possessive term of mild condescension is a good character beat. 'Chugots' is pure Ilana verbal invention. The reversal — she's impatient with HIM for being slow when she's the one with an incomprehensible financial arrangement — is a clean irony joke.

jokes.text: Ilana receives $8,265 and celebrates ecstatically. Brad: 'Come on. Grow up. What are you, 26 now?' Ilana: 'I'm 27.' Then: 'And what are you, like, a hot, ripped, uncut 50-year-old?' Brad: '47.' Ilana: 'Okay, fine.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is doing a full victory celebration in Brad's office.
jokes.punchline: Brad's 'grow up' is a reasonable adult response; Ilana's retaliation compliment ('hot, ripped, uncut 50-year-old') backfires when he's 47 — and her 'Okay, fine' accepts the correction as a minor inconvenience.
jokes.explanation: The age exchange is a nice quick bit — Ilana's flattery misfires on the age (50 vs 47) and she absorbs the correction without embarrassment. 'Okay, fine' as a response to being three years off in your flirtatious insult is very Ilana. Good economy.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'What are you, like, a hot, ripped, uncut 50-year-old?' / Brad: '47.' / Ilana: 'Okay, fine.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is telling Brad he should grow up too — but frames it as a compliment-adjacent observation
jokes.punchline: 'Hot, ripped, uncut 50-year-old' — Ilana's critique of Brad's age is inseparable from her attraction to him; she insults him with a compliment and then recalibrates when he gives the actual number ('47' / 'Okay, fine')
jokes.explanation: 'Okay, fine' after being corrected from 50 to 47 implies Ilana is rapidly adjusting her attraction-shame calculus in real time. 'Uncut' as a detail is a specifically Ilana observation — completely uncensored, anatomically specific, delivered as an aesthetic compliment. Good economy in the exchange.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I just mean that we had sex when I was 21. That's gross. You grow up, maybe.'
jokes.setup: Brad is defending his younger-woman relationship as having boosted his self-esteem during a divorce.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's verdict — 'That's gross. You grow up, maybe' — turns the tables, recasting her younger self as a victim of his insecurity rather than an enthusiastic participant she has nostalgically described.
jokes.explanation: A sharp character moment where Ilana's retroactive feminist reframing of her own past hookup is both funny and meaningful. 'You grow up, maybe' directed at a 47-year-old man by Ilana is very good — the reversal of his earlier 'grow up' is noted. Economy is high; she says a lot in one breath.

jokes.text: Brad mentions he's now in an 'age-appropriate relationship' with someone named Marie and thinks Ilana would like her. Ilana: 'Okay, that's cool. You're cool. You're good.'
jokes.setup: Brad shares that he's moved on to an appropriate relationship.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's rapid-fire affirmations — 'You're cool. You're good.' — are the verbal behavior of someone desperately trying to seem unbothered while being clearly bothered.
jokes.explanation: A familiar comedic beat — 'I'm totally fine' denial behavior — but well-executed for Ilana specifically. The triple affirmation ('Okay, that's cool. You're cool. You're good.') has good rhythm. Not exceptional but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Ilana exits into a Bitcoin protest: 'No more Bitcoin! Bitcoin is killing the Earth!'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just cashed out her Bitcoin.
jokes.punchline: She immediately walks into a protest against the thing she just celebrated — the timing is perfect dramatic irony.
jokes.explanation: The structural irony is very clean: Ilana exits her Bitcoin triumph directly into an anti-Bitcoin protest. The show doesn't editorialize — it just lets the timing do the work. Good comedic structure.

jokes.text: Protester explains Bitcoin: 'It has to be mined by thousands of computers 24/7... it's the pedophile's currency of choice. Fueling the child-sex-trade industry and dominating the illegal-arms world.'
jokes.setup: The protester is explaining why Bitcoin is bad.
jokes.punchline: The environmental argument escalates to pedophile currency, sex-trade fuel, and illegal arms — each accusation delivered with activist earnestness.
jokes.explanation: The Bitcoin critique escalation is funny because the protester is both correct (on the energy) and spiraling into the darkest possible framing of cryptocurrency. 'Pedophile's currency of choice' as a protest chyron is a bold comedic choice. The character is funny as the activist whose passion slightly exceeds his messaging discipline.

jokes.text: Protester: 'Well, you can thank Bitcoin for that, 'cause it's contributing to climate change, so you're feeling the effects.' Ilana: 'Bitcoin is killing the Earth!'
jokes.setup: Ilana complains it's hot outside.
jokes.punchline: The protester attributes the current weather to Bitcoin specifically, and Ilana immediately adopts the entire worldview and joins the chant.
jokes.explanation: Ilana's immediate conversion — from Bitcoin celebrant to Bitcoin protestor, in real time, simply because someone made a compelling point — is perfect character comedy. She's not hypocritical; she's genuinely persuadable and enthusiastic about the new worldview. Her joining the chant is the logical endpoint.

jokes.text: Ilana asks: 'Is it hot... hot out to you?' Protester: 'Well, you can thank Bitcoin for that, 'cause it's contributing to climate change.'
jokes.setup: Ilana, wearing full patent leather in summer heat, notices she's uncomfortably warm
jokes.punchline: The protester immediately attributes Ilana's personal discomfort to Bitcoin's contribution to climate change — a macro accusation applied to a micro moment of sweating in leather
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured joke. Ilana is literally feeling the heat of her financial decision (sweating in patent leather bought for the Bitcoin meeting) and the protester connects her discomfort to climate change caused by Bitcoin — collapsing personal and planetary consequence into one moment. The visual gag (she can barely move in the leather) feeds the joke.

jokes.text: Ilana and Abbi's rapid-fire guilt spiral: 'What about eating meat? The fashion industry burns remainder clothes at the end of every season. It's all made by little children. Recycling's a hoax.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks if she needs to donate her Bitcoin money given that it's evil.
jokes.punchline: The pair competitively list environmental and ethical disasters to dilute the guilt — each revelation more grim than the last — until the original question is buried.
jokes.explanation: The 'whataboutism as guilt management' bit is a good observational piece — the two friends genuinely work through every possible moral deflection to justify keeping the Bitcoin money. The 'Garbage Island' and 'Mother Earth is like Noooo!' beats add to the escalating absurdism. This is a solid comedy-duo sequence with good rhythm.

jokes.text: Ilana's spiraling environmental guilt: meat industry, fashion burning clothes, recycling hoax, 'Garbage Island is real'
jokes.setup: Ilana is guilt-spiraling about whether she has to give back the Bitcoin money given its environmental impact
jokes.punchline: Rather than resolve the Bitcoin question, she and Abbi spin out into a full accounting of every environmental crisis — meat, fashion, recycling, Garbage Island — each accusation canceling out the last in an expanding guilt vortex
jokes.explanation: The joke is that every ethical objection is countered with a bigger ethical failure, creating a spiral that makes action impossible — which is the point. Ilana isn't avoiding responsibility; she's genuinely trying to do the utilitarian calculus. The specificity ('udon containers,' 'Garbage Island') keeps it grounded. Abbi's 'Mother Earth is like Noooo!' is the absurdist peak.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'You really scrubbing out my udon containers and peeling the labels off and melting the plastic down to re-form them into new udon containers?'
jokes.setup: The conversation has turned to whether recycling actually works
jokes.punchline: Ilana's extremely specific description of what 'proper recycling' would entail — scrubbing, peeling labels, melting — is both accurate as a critique of recycling's limitations and funny because of 'udon containers' as the specific item
jokes.explanation: 'Udon containers' is the specific detail that makes this line sing. The observation itself (recycling is a myth) was already becoming a common discourse point by 2018-2019, but the udon specificity makes it feel lived-in and personal.

jokes.text: 'Mother Earth is like, "Noooo! Noo!" And we're like, "No, bitch, die, bitch!"'
jokes.setup: Ilana is describing the human relationship to the environment.
jokes.punchline: She ventriloquizes both Mother Earth and humanity in an exchange where humanity is casually genocidal toward the planet, delivering it with cheerful earnestness.
jokes.explanation: 'No, bitch, die, bitch' as humanity's position in the climate debate is a great absurdist capsule — it takes the political complexity of climate inaction and renders it as a casual drive-by. Ilana's tone (not angry, just explanatory) makes it funnier. High character integration because this is Ilana's exact rhetorical register.

jokes.text: 'Why is the news not covering this 24/7?' Abbi: 'Don't even get me started on the news.' Then Abbi refers to 'some stupid but really understandable layout issues on fliers. Like, what?'
jokes.setup: The two are ranting about the news media failing to cover real issues.
jokes.punchline: Abbi slips in a self-referential critique of the news covering her 'missing person' story — framing it as a 'really understandable layout issue' while condemning media priorities — acknowledging her own role in the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: A good self-aware callback where Abbi weaves her own subplot into a larger media critique — while still defending her graphic design error as 'understandable.' The joke rewards viewers who've been tracking the episode's threads and works as a moment of both self-awareness and self-justification.

jokes.text: 'We are being listened to all the time. Anything that we do on social media for free, we pay for through our experience. We are just data.'
jokes.setup: The guilt spiral has expanded to include surveillance capitalism.
jokes.punchline: Abbi makes a genuinely correct and dark point about data privacy — which lands as comedy because of the context (two friends justifying Bitcoin money) and the sudden sincerity.
jokes.explanation: The data-privacy point is real but not especially funny in itself — the comedy is contextual rather than intrinsic to the line. This is the weakest beat in the guilt-spiral sequence; the observation doesn't have a comic twist, just accurate doom.

jokes.text: 'Whew, I feel so much better. Thank you so much.'
jokes.setup: They've just catalogued every possible environmental and ethical disaster.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's relief — having done zero to address any of the issues — is the joke. The spiral of guilt has somehow resolved into catharsis.
jokes.explanation: A solid button on the guilt-spiral sequence. 'Whew, I feel so much better' after having resolved nothing is funny because it's accurate — the naming of all the problems as a substitute for addressing them is exactly how millennial liberal guilt works. Economy is strong; the line does precise character work.

jokes.text: Ilana notes the Phone Wig symbol is 'an ancient Jewish symbol... a Jewish thing. It's like a mystical, tribal Jewish...' then stops herself: 'Hold on. Just say the thing.' Then she delivers her sincere commitment speech.
jokes.setup: Ilana is building to a sincere promise about following through with the Patent.
jokes.punchline: She almost derails her own emotional moment with a digression about Jewish symbolism, then course-corrects by telling herself to 'just say the thing' — an unusual moment of Ilana self-editing.
jokes.explanation: Ilana catching herself spiraling mid-speech and instructing herself to 'just say the thing' is a nice meta-moment — she's aware of her own patterns. The Jewish symbolism digression is a perfectly Ilana tangent. Character integration is very high.

jokes.text: Ilana's sincere commitment speech ends with: 'I'm gonna be investing in a woman-owned wiz-ness, and that's gonna tip the scales of progress. And it uses biodegradable materials.' Abbi: 'Human hair!' Ilana: 'Ugh, I love that.'
jokes.setup: Ilana delivers an earnest feminist business manifesto.
jokes.punchline: The biodegradable material turns out to be human hair — a detail that is both genuinely on-brand for Phone Wigs and also deeply unsettling — and Ilana's 'Ugh, I love that' treats it as an aesthetic triumph.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Phone Wigs are biodegradable because they use human hair is a great comedic button — it makes Ilana's environmental justification both correct and deeply weird. 'Ugh, I love that' as Ilana's response to the horror of human-hair phone cases is very on-character.

jokes.text: Phone Wigs are biodegradable — 'Human hair!' — celebrated as an environmental win
jokes.setup: Ilana has just committed to investing in Phone Wigs as an ethical use of the Bitcoin money
jokes.punchline: She reveals Phone Wigs are biodegradable because they're made of human hair — the environmental win that resolves the guilt spiral is that her product involves harvesting human hair for phone cases
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Phone Wigs solve the eco-guilt problem because they use human hair is genuinely absurdist and structurally satisfying. The environmental spiral (Bitcoin → meat → fashion → recycling → guilt) is resolved by Ilana's existing business accidentally being the most ethical option — because it's made of human body material. 'Human hair!' delivered as a triumphant exclamation closes the loop. Earns 8+ as a structural payoff with strong character integration.

jokes.text: Final gag: Ilana's patent leather outfit has adhered to her skin. The solution: lubricant. 'We could try again with almond oil.' Ilana: 'Feh.'
jokes.setup: The episode wraps up with Ilana's Bitcoin/patent leather outfit from the whole episode.
jokes.punchline: The outfit has literally bonded to her body, requiring lubricant to remove — a physical comedy endpoint to the patent leather joke, topped with Ilana's disgusted 'Feh' as a closer.
jokes.explanation: A physical comedy button that closes the outfit arc. The almond oil solution is a good absurdist detail — very specific, very Broad City. 'Feh' as Ilana's final word is a characteristically understated exit. A solid closer but not the episode's strongest moment.
